originalTitle: Women secretly filmed, then ridiculed and abused online
title: Your Pictures, Your Choice! Let's Be Kind Online!
content:
Hello, wonderful children of Africa! It's your friend here, ready to share some important news with you. We live in a world where we can connect with so many people. We use phones and computers to see fun videos and learn new things. But sometimes, not everyone uses these tools in a kind way. We need to talk about how to be safe and kind when we are online.

What Happened to Our Friends?

Some grown-up ladies in two beautiful African countries, Kenya and Ghana, told a big news company about something sad. A man came to them and secretly took videos. 'Secretly' means he did it without them knowing, like a little surprise, but not a happy one. He did not ask them if it was okay. After taking these videos, he put them on the internet. He shared them with many, many people. And then, some people started saying mean things about the ladies in the videos. They made fun of them. This made the ladies feel very sad, embarrassed, and even scared. Nobody likes it when people are unkind. Did you know? Kenya and Ghana are countries in Africa, just like where you might live! They have lots of amazing things, like beautiful animals and wonderful people.

What is 'Asking Permission'?

When you want to play with your friend's toy, what do you do? You ask, "Can I play with your car?" or "May I use your crayons?" Right? It's polite and kind. This is what we call 'asking permission'. It means asking someone if something is okay before you do it. It's super important to ask permission before taking a picture or a video of someone. Imagine someone taking your picture when you don't want them to, and then showing it to everyone! That wouldn't feel nice, would it? The ladies in Kenya and Ghana did not give permission for their videos to be taken or shared. Did you know? Asking permission is a way to show respect. It means you care about how other people feel!

What is 'Online' and the Internet?

We talk a lot about 'online' and the 'internet'. What are they? Imagine a super-duper big network, like a giant spiderweb made of invisible strings! These strings connect computers and phones all over the world. When you use your mom's phone to watch a cartoon, you are often 'online'. When you see pictures on a screen, they might be from the internet. The internet is amazing! You can learn about animals, see pictures of faraway places, and even talk to family who live far away. It's like a magical library and a play area all in one. But, just like in a playground, sometimes there are rules to follow, and sometimes, people might not be playing nicely. Did you know? Billions of people all around the world use the internet every day! That's a huge number, like all the ants in your garden, but many, many more!

Why is This Not Okay?

Taking videos of someone without asking, and then sharing them, is not okay for many reasons. First, it takes away someone's privacy. 'Privacy' means having a space that belongs only to you, like your own room or your own thoughts. Your pictures and videos are part of your private space. When the man shared the videos, people started saying mean things. This is called 'ridiculing' or 'abusing' online. It's like teasing someone very badly, but on the internet. It can make people feel very, very small and sad. Nobody deserves to be treated that way. Did you know? Your feelings are important! If something makes you sad or uncomfortable, it's okay to say so.

What Can We Learn from This?

This story teaches us some really big lessons about being good digital citizens. A 'digital citizen' is like a good neighbour in the internet world. 1. **Always Ask Permission:** Before you take a picture or video of anyone, even your friends or family, ask them, "Is it okay if I take your picture?" If they say no, then don't do it. It's their choice. 2. **Be Kind Online:** Think before you type or share. If you wouldn't say it to someone's face, don't say it online. Let's make the internet a happy and friendly place. 3. **If You See Something Mean, Tell a Grown-up:** If you ever see a video or picture online that makes someone sad, or if someone is saying unkind things, tell your mom, dad, teacher, or another trusted grown-up right away. They can help! Did you know? Everything you put on the internet, like pictures or words, can stay there for a very long time! So, it's good to share only kind and happy things.

Who Can Help Us?

Your parents, guardians, teachers, and other trusted adults are there to keep you safe. If you ever feel worried or scared about something you see online, or if someone asks you to do something you don't like, please tell a grown-up immediately. They will listen to you and help you. They want you to be safe and happy, always. Let's all be superheroes of kindness online! Let's use the internet to learn, play, and make friends, but always with respect and care for everyone around us, both in our homes and all over the world. Remember, your pictures are your choice, and being kind is always the best choice!
summary: Some women in Kenya and Ghana were secretly filmed by a man who then put their videos online, where people said mean things about them, making them sad and scared.
whyMatters: This story teaches us that we must always ask for permission before taking someone's picture or video, be kind online, and tell a grown-up if something bad happens on the internet, so everyone can feel safe and respected.

originalTitle: South Africa's exclusion from G7 summit no surprise, says Ramaphosa
title: Our President and the Big World Meeting!
content:
Hello, Wonderful Friends!

Hi everyone! It's me, your friendly news reporter for Africa! I have an interesting story to share with you today. It's about our amazing continent, Africa, and especially about South Africa, a very special country with lots of sunshine and friendly faces. We are going to talk about a big meeting that happened far away, and why our own President from South Africa, Mr. Cyril Ramaphosa, wasn't there. It's like when you have a big party at school, and sometimes not everyone can come. But don't worry, it's not a sad story! It's about how leaders talk to make our world a better place.

What is the G7 Meeting?

Imagine a group of very important grown-ups. These grown-ups are the leaders of some very big and powerful countries in the world. They come together to talk about important things. They discuss how to help people, how to keep everyone safe, and how to make sure we all have enough food and clean water. This special meeting is called the G7. The "G" stands for "Group" and the "7" means there are seven main countries in this group. They are like a team that works together to solve big world problems. **Did you know?** The G7 countries are Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, the United Kingdom, and the United States. That's a lot of different places!

President Ramaphosa and the Invitation

So, sometimes, even though the G7 is a group of seven countries, they invite other important leaders to join their special talks. It's like inviting extra friends to your birthday party! Our very own President Cyril Ramaphosa, who is the leader of South Africa, was going to be invited to this big meeting. He was getting ready to pack his bags and travel far away to France, which is where the meeting was happening this time. It would have been a wonderful chance for him to share ideas and speak up for Africa.

A Little Mix-Up

But then, something happened. It was a bit like when you make plans with your friends, and then someone else changes the plan. Reports said that a very important leader from one of the G7 countries, the United States, had some different ideas. This leader, Mr. Donald Trump, said that if President Ramaphosa came to the meeting, he might not come himself. It sounds a bit complicated, right? But what it meant was that the country hosting the meeting, France, decided to take back President Ramaphosa's invitation. **Did you know?** Leaders from different countries often have different ideas about things. But they usually try to talk things out and find solutions together!

Why Did This Happen?

It's hard to know exactly why things like this happen. Sometimes, big countries have different opinions about what is best for the world. Maybe they didn't agree on everything. What we do know is that President Ramaphosa himself said that it was "no surprise" that he wasn't there. This means he probably understood that sometimes plans change, especially when many big leaders are involved. He knows that South Africa is still very important, even if it doesn't get to go to every single meeting.

Why is it Important for South Africa?

Even though President Ramaphosa didn't go to this G7 meeting, it's still very important for South Africa to be part of these big world discussions. When our President goes to meetings, he speaks for all of us. He can tell other leaders about the amazing things happening in Africa and the challenges we face. He can make new friends for South Africa and find ways to work together with other countries. This helps us to grow, get new schools, better hospitals, and make sure everyone has a good life. **Did you know?** South Africa is often called the "Rainbow Nation" because we have so many different cultures and languages living together happily!

South Africa Keeps Working Hard!

So, even without going to this one specific meeting, South Africa continues to be a strong and important country. President Ramaphosa and our government keep working very hard to make friends with other countries all over the world. They meet with leaders from other parts of Africa, from Europe, from Asia, and everywhere! These connections are super important because they help us share ideas, trade goods, and learn from each other.

Wrapping Up

So, there you have it! A story about a big world meeting, an invitation that changed, and our strong President. Remember, even when plans change, South Africa is a wonderful country filled with brilliant people. We will always find ways to make our voices heard and work together to build a brighter future for everyone. Thanks for listening, and keep being curious about our amazing world!
summary: South Africa's President, Mr. Cyril Ramaphosa, was going to attend a big meeting of important country leaders called the G7, but his invitation was taken back after a leader from the USA had a different idea.
whyMatters: This is important because it shows how leaders from different countries talk and sometimes disagree, but South Africa still works hard to make friends and share its ideas with the world to make life better for everyone.

originalTitle: Rationing power and diluting petrol - how African countries are coping with effects of Iran war
title: Lights, Cars, and Helping Hands: How Africa is Saving Energy!
content:
Hello, Super Kids of Africa!

Hi everyone! It's your friendly news reporter, here to share some important news from our amazing continent, Africa! I know sometimes grown-ups talk about big words like "war" or "fuel shortages," and it can sound a bit confusing or even scary. But don't worry! My job is to explain things in a super simple way, just for you, my smart friends in Class 2. We're going to talk about something that helps our lights shine and our cars go, and how we can all be superheroes by saving a little bit of it.

What is This "Fuel" We Hear About?

Imagine your favourite toy car. To make it go really fast, sometimes it needs special batteries, right? Well, big cars, buses, and even the machines that make electricity for our homes need a special kind of "juice" too. We call this "fuel" or sometimes "oil." This oil comes from deep, deep underground, like a hidden treasure! It's used for many things:
  • To make cars, trucks, and buses move.
  • To help big power stations make electricity so our lights turn on and our TVs work.
  • To cook food in some homes.
**Did you know?** Oil was made from tiny plants and animals that lived in the sea millions and millions of years ago! It's like nature's very old, special energy drink!

A Big Problem Far Away

Sometimes, in places very far away from us, grown-ups have big arguments. These big arguments can sometimes turn into something we call a "war." Right now, there's a war happening in a country called Iran. It's very far, far away from Africa. But even though it's far, it can still affect us a little bit. How? Well, this war is making it difficult for countries to get the "fuel" or "oil" they need. It's like if your favourite shop suddenly ran out of your favourite juice. When there's less fuel, it becomes harder for our countries in Africa to get enough for our cars and our lights.

How Does It Affect Our Homes?

So, when there's less fuel, what happens? **Lights Out Sometimes:** You might have noticed that sometimes the lights or the TV suddenly turn off. This is called a "power cut" or "load shedding." It happens because the power stations that make electricity don't have enough fuel to make electricity all the time. So, they have to switch off the power in some places for a little while to save what they have. It's like sharing a small cake – everyone gets a piece, but maybe not as much as they usually do. **Cars and Buses:** Your mom or dad might have to wait a bit longer to fill up the car with fuel. Or maybe they talk about fuel being more expensive. This is because there isn't as much fuel, so it costs more to buy. Some countries are trying to make the fuel last longer by mixing it with other things, but this can sometimes make cars not work as well. The best thing is to use less fuel! **Did you know?** Many countries in Africa are super sunny! We can use the sun to make electricity, which is called solar power. The sun is a giant power station for free!

What Are Our Grown-Ups Doing?

Our leaders and grown-ups in Africa are working very hard to solve this problem. They are like super problem-solvers! **Saving Electricity:** Many countries are asking everyone to save electricity. Big buildings might turn off some lights at night. They want us to remember to switch off lights and fans when we leave a room. **Finding Other Energy:** They are also looking for new ways to make electricity. Instead of just using fuel from underground, they are trying to use the sun's power (solar power) or the wind's power (wind power). These are clean and never run out! **Did you know?** Wind turbines, which look like giant fans, can make electricity when the wind blows! It's another way to get clean energy. **Talking to Other Countries:** Our grown-ups are also talking to other friendly countries around the world to see if they can help us get more fuel. They are working together, just like good friends help each other.

What Can YOU Do to Help?

Even though you are small, you are super important! You can be an amazing energy-saving superhero! Every little bit helps. Here are some easy things you can do:
  1. **Switch Off the Lights:** When you leave a room, make sure to switch off the lights. Do you really need all the lights on during the day if the sun is shining brightly outside? No!
  2. **Unplug Things:** When you finish charging your mom's phone or playing with a toy that plugs into the wall, ask a grown-up to unplug it. Even when a charger is not charging, it still uses a tiny bit of electricity!
  3. **Turn Off the TV:** If no one is watching the TV, switch it off. Maybe you can read a book or play outside instead!
  4. **Short Showers:** Try to take quick showers. Sometimes, electricity is used to heat the water or pump it to your house.
  5. **Walk or Cycle:** If a place is not too far, ask your parents if you can walk or ride your bicycle instead of going by car. It's good for you and saves fuel!
  6. **Close the Fridge Door:** Don't leave the fridge door open for too long! The fridge uses a lot of electricity to stay cold.
  7. **Be a Remind-Her/Him:** Gently remind your grown-ups or siblings to save energy too! You can be the family's energy-saving champion!
**Did you know?** When you ride your bicycle, you are using your own body's energy instead of fuel! You are like a super energy machine!

Together, We Are Strong!

See? Even a big problem far away can be managed when we all work together. Our countries in Africa are strong, and our people are clever. By being smart about how we use electricity and fuel, we can all help. Remember, every time you switch off a light or save water, you are doing something amazing for our continent. You are a true African hero! Keep shining bright, my wonderful friends!
summary: A war far away is making it harder for African countries to get enough fuel and electricity, but everyone is working together to save energy and find new ways to power our homes.
whyMatters: This matters because energy helps our lights shine, our cars go, and keeps our food fresh, so saving it helps everyone in our community and keeps our beautiful continent moving forward.

originalTitle: 'My daughter is under the rubble': Inside Tehran as civilian toll of strikes rises
title: A Story About Our Friends Far Away in Iran
content:
Hello, super friends! It's me, your friendly reporter from Africa! Today, we're going to talk about something happening very far away, in a country called Iran. It's a bit of a sad story, but it's important to learn about it with kind hearts and understand what our friends there are going through.

Where is Iran?

Iran is a big country. It's very far from where we live in Africa. It's in a place called Asia. Imagine looking at a big, big map of the world. You would see it there! It has mountains, deserts, and many big cities, just like some places here in Africa.

What Happened in Iran?

Imagine your street. Imagine your house. Now imagine big, loud noises, like thunder, but much, much louder. These loud noises were called "strikes." These strikes caused some houses and buildings to break. It made people very, very scared. This happened about one month ago. Grown-ups from some other big countries had very big disagreements. Because of these disagreements, these strikes happened in Iran. These loud noises made many people in the city of Tehran feel very unsafe and sad.

Who Lives in Iran?

Just like us, families live in Iran. There are moms, dads, grandmas, grandpas, brothers, and sisters. There are children, just like you! They go to school. They play games. They have toys. They love to laugh and spend time with their families. The big city we are talking about is called Tehran. It's a very big city in Iran. Lots and lots of people live there. They wake up, they eat breakfast, they go to work or school, just like we do every day.

Homes and Families Hurt

When the strikes happened, some homes broke. Some buildings fell down. This made many people very, very sad. Imagine your favorite toy breaking, or your bed, or your kitchen table. Now imagine your whole house breaking down. Some moms and dads told news reporters, "Our homes are broken!" One mom was very sad. She said, "My daughter is under the rubble." "Rubble" means all the broken pieces of a building, like bricks and wood. It means her daughter was hurt and stuck. This is very, very sad news for a mother to share.

Did you know?

Did you know that children all over the world, no matter where they live, love to play? They love hide-and-seek, tag, and drawing pictures. Children in Iran love to play too! They also enjoy stories and playing with their friends.

Why Are People Sad?

When homes break, people lose their things. They lose their beds, their clothes, their books, and their toys. More importantly, they might lose their loved ones. Some people got hurt. Some people lost their families or friends. This is why their hearts are so heavy. Their lives feel very, very hard now. It's like a big, sad storm came and made everything messy and broken. They feel sad and worried about what will happen next, and where they will live.

What is a Civilian?

A "civilian" is a normal person. It's not a soldier who wears a uniform and fights. It's people like your mom, your dad, your teacher, or your neighbor. It's children, like you! They are just living their daily lives. When we hear about "civilian toll," it means that normal people, not just soldiers, got hurt or lost their lives. This is always very sad because these people just want to live their peaceful lives, go to work, and raise their families.

Did you know?

Did you know that the word "peace" means when everyone is calm and happy, and there are no fights or loud noises from disagreements? We all wish for peace so that everyone can be safe.

What Happens After Such Sadness?

After something like this, people need lots of help. They need new places to sleep, because their homes are broken. They need food to eat, and clean water to drink. They need doctors to help people who got hurt. They also need kind friends to listen to them and help them feel a little better. Helpers are trying very hard to clear the broken pieces. They are trying to find people who are stuck. They are trying to make things better. It takes a very long time for a city to heal when its buildings are broken and its people are sad.

Did you know?

Did you know that "Tehran" is the capital city of Iran? A capital city is like the main, most important city in a country! It's usually where the government works and where many people live and work.

Hoping for Kindness and Peace

It is hard to hear about people far away feeling sad and hurt. But it helps us remember to be kind to each other every day. We can be kind to our friends, our family, and even people we don't know very well, whether they live next door or in a country far away. When grown-ups fight, children everywhere feel the sadness. We all want the world to be a safe place for everyone. We want smiles, not tears. We want houses, not rubble. We want everyone to feel safe and loved.

Did you know?

Did you know that one way to help people who are sad is just to listen to them and show them you care? Even a small act of kindness, like drawing a happy picture for someone, can make a big difference!

What Can We Do?

Even though we are far away in Africa, we can think kind thoughts for the children and families in Iran. We can hope that they get all the help they need. We can hope that peace comes back to their city very, very soon. We can also try to make sure that in our own schools and homes, we always choose to be kind and solve our problems with words, not with big disagreements. Let's send big, warm, kind thoughts to our friends in Iran. We hope they can soon play and laugh again, with safe homes and happy families. Thank you for listening, my super friends!
summary: Friends in Iran, a country far away, are feeling very sad because loud noises and attacks broke their homes and hurt their families, and they need help.
whyMatters: This story matters because it shows us that big disagreements between grown-ups can hurt regular people and children, reminding us to be kind and hope for peace everywhere so everyone can live safely.
